EARS-001.description: State requirements with no trigger and no state as a Ubiquitous requirement using the pattern 'THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>', for behavior that must always hold true.
trigger_criteria: Requirement describes a continuously true property/behavior (no precondition) but is not written as 'THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>'
Requirement is written as ubiquitous but actually depends on an unstated trigger or state, which should instead use EARS-002/003/004
EARS-001.example_compliant: THE VEHICLE SPEED MODULE SHALL process the vehicle speed signal every [20 ms].
EARS-001.disambiguation: Use only for behavior with no conditional trigger and no state qualifier. If a trigger or state exists, use EARS-002 (event-driven), EARS-003 (unwanted behavior), or EARS-004 (state-driven) instead.

EARS-002.description: State requirements triggered by a detectable event using the pattern 'WHEN <trigger>, THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement describes a system response to a discrete, instantaneous event but omits the 'WHEN' keyword or omits the trigger clause entirely
Trigger is expressed with a different keyword (e.g. 'IF') when the underlying intent is a normal/expected event rather than an unwanted condition
EARS-002.example_violation: The system shall deploy the airbag on impact detection.
EARS-002.example_compliant: WHEN the crash sensor detects an impact exceeding [30 g], THE RESTRAINT CONTROL MODULE SHALL deploy the airbag within [20 ms].
EARS-002.disambiguation: Reserve 'WHEN' for expected, in-scope events. If the trigger represents a fault or undesired condition, use EARS-003 (unwanted behavior) with 'IF...THEN' instead.

EARS-003.description: State requirements responding to an undesired or abnormal condition using the pattern 'IF <trigger>, THEN THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement describes a fault, failure, or abnormal condition and its required response, but does not use the 'IF...THEN' construction
'WHEN' is used for what is clearly a fault/error condition instead of 'IF...THEN'
EARS-003.example_violation: When VehSpd is out of range, the system shall set a DTC.
EARS-003.example_compliant: IF the vehicle speed signal is out of range [0 kph, 250 kph], THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL set the diagnostic trouble code for vehicle speed out-of-range within [100 ms].
EARS-003.disambiguation: Distinct from EARS-002: use 'IF...THEN' specifically for undesired/abnormal triggers (faults, errors, out-of-range values); use 'WHEN' (EARS-002) for normal, expected events.

EARS-004.description: State requirements that apply only while the system is in a specific, verifiable state using the pattern 'WHILE <state>, THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement's applicability depends on a continuous system state or mode (e.g. 'Drive mode', 'engine running', 'ignition on') but does not use the 'WHILE' keyword
State condition is implied by context rather than explicitly stated in the requirement
EARS-004.example_violation: In Drive mode, the system processes the brake pedal position.
EARS-004.example_compliant: WHILE the transmission is in Drive mode, THE BRAKE CONTROL MODULE SHALL process the brake pedal position signal within [10 ms].
EARS-004.disambiguation: Use 'WHILE' only for a state that persists over an interval and can be objectively verified as active or inactive at any point in time; a one-time occurrence belongs under EARS-002 or EARS-003, not this pattern.

EARS-005.description: State requirements that apply only when an optional feature is present in the product configuration using the pattern 'WHERE <feature is included>, THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement's applicability depends on an optional/configurable feature (e.g. adaptive cruise control, heated seats) but does not use the 'WHERE' keyword
'WHERE' clause names a runtime state/mode rather than a build-time/configuration option (should instead be EARS-004)
EARS-005.example_violation: For vehicles with adaptive cruise control, the system shall maintain the set following distance.
EARS-005.example_compliant: WHERE the Adaptive Cruise Control feature is installed, THE ACC MODULE SHALL maintain the set following distance within [+/-0.5 s].
EARS-005.disambiguation: Distinct from EARS-004: 'WHERE' identifies a configuration-time option present or absent for a given vehicle build; 'WHILE' (EARS-004) identifies a runtime operating state that changes during vehicle operation.

EARS-006.description: State requirements combining state, optional feature, and/or trigger clauses using the full EARS complex/hybrid pattern, e.g. 'WHERE <feature>, WHILE <state>, WHEN <trigger>, THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>'.
trigger_criteria: Requirement legitimately combines more than one condition type (state + trigger, or feature + state) but does not clearly separate/label each clause with its correct EARS keyword
Combined clauses are run together as prose rather than as distinct, keyword-tagged clauses
EARS-006.example_violation: If the vehicle has ACC and is in Drive mode and the lead vehicle brakes suddenly, the system shall apply the brakes.
EARS-006.example_compliant: WHERE the Adaptive Cruise Control feature is installed, WHILE the system is in Drive mode, WHEN the lead vehicle deceleration exceeds [0.3 g], THE ACC MODULE SHALL apply the service brakes within [200 ms].
EARS-006.disambiguation: Only use this pattern when two or more condition types genuinely co-occur. If a single condition type suffices, prefer the simpler EARS-002/003/004/005 pattern instead of forcing a hybrid structure.

EARS-007.description: Select the EARS keyword (WHEN, IF, WHILE, WHERE) that matches the semantic nature of the condition: event, fault, state, or optional feature respectively.
trigger_criteria: 'WHEN' is used for a fault/abnormal condition (should be 'IF...THEN')
'IF...THEN' is used for a normal, expected event (should be 'WHEN')
'WHILE' is used for a discrete one-time event rather than a persistent state (should be 'WHEN')
'WHERE' is used for a runtime condition rather than an optional configuration item (should be 'WHILE')
EARS-007.example_violation: WHEN the coolant temperature sensor fails, THE SYSTEM SHALL illuminate the warning lamp.
EARS-007.example_compliant: IF the coolant temperature sensor signal is invalid, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL illuminate the coolant warning lamp within [500 ms].
EARS-007.disambiguation: This is a keyword-semantics check distinct from EARS-001 through EARS-006, which check overall pattern structure. A requirement can have the right overall shape but still use the wrong keyword for its condition type.

EARS-008.description: Use 'SHALL' exactly once per requirement statement to express the single mandatory system response; do not use 'should', 'will', 'must', or multiple 'SHALL' clauses joined together.
trigger_criteria: Statement uses 'should', 'will', 'must', or 'may' where a mandatory requirement is intended instead of 'SHALL'
Statement contains two or more 'SHALL' clauses joined by 'and'/'or', indicating more than one requirement packed into one statement
EARS-008.example_violation: THE SYSTEM SHALL detect the fault and it must set the DTC.
EARS-008.example_compliant: THE SYSTEM SHALL detect the fault within [10 ms].
EARS-008.disambiguation: Overlaps with EARS-016 (single response action): EARS-008 focuses narrowly on modal-verb word choice and count of 'SHALL' occurrences; EARS-016 focuses on whether the response clause bundles multiple distinct actions.

EARS-009.description: Identify the responsible system, subsystem, or component explicitly as the subject of the SHALL clause, using a name consistent with the project architecture.
trigger_criteria: Subject is missing, implied, or replaced by a pronoun ('it shall...')
Subject is more generic than the architecture warrants (e.g. 'THE VEHICLE SHALL' when only the 'BRAKE CONTROL MODULE' is responsible)
EARS-009.example_violation: WHEN the brake pedal is pressed, it shall apply the brake force.
EARS-009.example_compliant: WHEN the brake pedal position exceeds [10%], THE BRAKE CONTROL MODULE SHALL apply the requested brake force within [50 ms].
EARS-009.disambiguation: Distinct from EARS-007 (keyword selection): this rule concerns the subject/actor named after the keyword clause, not which keyword is chosen.

EARS-010.description: Every need or requirement statement must fit one of the five EARS patterns (Ubiquitous, Event-Driven, Unwanted Behavior, State-Driven, Optional Feature) or their Complex combination; avoid free-form statements outside these patterns.
trigger_criteria: Statement is a fragment, note, or description that does not map to any EARS pattern
Statement contains a SHALL clause but no identifiable pattern keyword structure where one is warranted (e.g. a conditional buried in prose rather than expressed via WHEN/IF/WHILE/WHERE)
EARS-010.example_violation: Vehicle speed processing needs to happen fast, especially during braking.
EARS-010.example_compliant: WHILE the brake pedal is applied, THE VEHICLE SPEED MODULE SHALL process the vehicle speed signal within [10 ms].
EARS-010.disambiguation: This is the EARS-set counterpart to INCOSE-001 (structured_statements); use this rule specifically when the statement fails to map onto any of the five EARS patterns, not for generic template non-conformance.

EARS-011.description: In event-driven requirements, place the 'WHEN <trigger>' clause before the 'THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>' clause.
trigger_criteria: The response (SHALL clause) is stated before the WHEN trigger clause
The WHEN clause is embedded mid-sentence after the SHALL action rather than leading the statement
EARS-011.example_violation: THE SYSTEM SHALL illuminate the low-fuel indicator when the fuel level drops below [10%].
EARS-011.example_compliant: WHEN the fuel level drops below [10%], THE INSTRUMENT CLUSTER SHALL illuminate the low-fuel indicator within [1 s].
EARS-011.disambiguation: Ordering-only check. If the trigger itself is a fault/abnormal condition, this rule still applies but the keyword should be 'IF...THEN' per EARS-003/EARS-007, not 'WHEN'.

EARS-012.description: In unwanted-behavior requirements, place the 'IF <trigger>' clause before the 'THEN THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>' clause, with 'THEN' marking the start of the response.
trigger_criteria: The 'IF' clause is present but 'THEN' is omitted before the SHALL clause
The action clause precedes the IF condition in the sentence
EARS-012.example_violation: THE SYSTEM SHALL set the over-voltage DTC if the battery voltage exceeds [16 V].
EARS-012.example_compliant: IF the battery voltage exceeds [16 V], THEN THE BODY CONTROL MODULE SHALL set the over-voltage diagnostic trouble code within [200 ms].
EARS-012.disambiguation: Distinct from EARS-011: this rule applies specifically to the IF...THEN unwanted-behavior pattern, including the requirement to keep the explicit 'THEN' marker, which EARS-011's WHEN pattern does not require.

EARS-013.description: Express each requirement with a single trigger or condition clause; do not combine multiple independent WHEN/IF triggers into one statement.
trigger_criteria: Two or more independent WHEN/IF trigger clauses are joined by 'and'/'or' or comma-separated within a single requirement, each warranting its own response
EARS-013.example_violation: WHEN the ignition is turned on and the driver door is opened, THE SYSTEM SHALL sound the seatbelt chime.
EARS-013.example_compliant: WHEN the ignition is turned on, THE SYSTEM SHALL sound the seatbelt chime if the seatbelt is unbuckled, within [2 s]. (driver-door-open case stated as a separate requirement)
EARS-013.disambiguation: Overlaps with INCOSE-011/INCOSE-028 style multi-condition issues; apply this EARS-specific version when the compound conditions are each independently sufficient to trigger the response, indicating they should be split into separate requirements.

EARS-014.description: The state named in a 'WHILE' clause must be an objectively verifiable, bounded operating state of the system, not a vague or continuously-varying condition.
trigger_criteria: The WHILE clause names a state that cannot be objectively determined to be active/inactive (e.g. 'while driving normally', 'while conditions are safe')
The WHILE clause describes a momentary event rather than a persistent state
EARS-014.example_violation: WHILE driving safely, THE SYSTEM SHALL limit acceleration.
EARS-014.example_compliant: WHILE the vehicle speed is within [0 kph, 30 kph] AND the parking assist feature is active, THE SYSTEM SHALL limit acceleration to [0.2 g].
EARS-014.disambiguation: Complements EARS-004 (pattern structure); this rule inspects the quality/verifiability of the state clause itself once the WHILE pattern has been correctly applied.

EARS-015.description: The 'WHERE' clause must name a specific, identifiable optional feature or configuration item, not a vague product tier or unstated option.
trigger_criteria: The WHERE clause references a vague grouping (e.g. 'premium trim', 'higher-spec vehicles') instead of the specific named optional feature
The WHERE clause is used without any corresponding entry in the feature/configuration list
EARS-015.example_violation: WHERE the vehicle is a premium trim, THE SYSTEM SHALL enable adaptive headlamps.
EARS-015.example_compliant: WHERE the Adaptive Headlamp feature is installed, THE LIGHTING CONTROL MODULE SHALL enable adaptive headlamp leveling.
EARS-015.disambiguation: Complements EARS-005 (pattern structure); this rule inspects whether the named feature is specific and traceable to a configuration item, not just whether the WHERE keyword is used.

EARS-016.description: Each requirement's SHALL clause should describe exactly one system response/action; list additional actions as separate requirements.
trigger_criteria: The SHALL clause lists two or more distinct actions joined by 'and'/commas (e.g. 'detect the fault, set the DTC, and illuminate the lamp')
EARS-016.example_violation: IF the oxygen sensor signal is out of range, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL set DTC_P0135 and illuminate the malfunction indicator lamp and log the freeze frame data.
EARS-016.example_compliant: IF the oxygen sensor signal is out of range, THEN THE ENGINE CONTROL MODULE SHALL set DTC_P0135 within [500 ms]. (illumination and freeze-frame logging stated as separate requirements)
EARS-016.disambiguation: This is the EARS-specific analogue of INCOSE-018 (single thought); apply specifically to the action list following SHALL, regardless of how the trigger/condition clause is formed.

EARS-017.description: The system response in the SHALL clause should include an explicit, measurable performance criterion (timing, tolerance, accuracy) sufficient for verification.
trigger_criteria: SHALL clause describes an action with no associated timing value, tolerance, or other measurable criterion where one is needed for verification
EARS-017.example_violation: WHEN the parking brake switch is engaged, THE SYSTEM SHALL illuminate the parking brake indicator.
EARS-017.example_compliant: WHEN the parking brake switch is engaged, THE INSTRUMENT CLUSTER SHALL illuminate the parking brake indicator within [200 ms].
EARS-017.disambiguation: Overlaps with INCOSE-034 (measurable_performance); apply the EARS-specific version when the missing criterion is attached directly to an EARS response clause following SHALL.

EARS-018.description: The SHALL clause should describe the required behavior/outcome, not a specific implementation mechanism, unless a design constraint rationale is provided.
trigger_criteria: SHALL clause specifies a particular algorithm, component part number, or internal mechanism instead of the required outcome, with no stated design-constraint rationale
EARS-018.example_violation: WHEN VehSpd exceeds [250 kph], THE SYSTEM SHALL execute the OverspeedHandler() function in the CAN gateway ECU.
EARS-018.example_compliant: WHEN the vehicle speed exceeds [250 kph], THE SYSTEM SHALL transition to the over-speed safe state within [50 ms].
EARS-018.disambiguation: This is the EARS-specific analogue of INCOSE-031 (solution_free); apply when the implementation detail appears specifically within the SHALL response clause.

EARS-019.description: When combining clause types in a complex/hybrid EARS requirement, order them as: WHERE (feature), WHILE (state), WHEN/IF (trigger), THE <SYSTEM> SHALL <ACTION>.
trigger_criteria: A hybrid requirement combines two or more clause types but in an order other than WHERE, WHILE, WHEN/IF, SHALL
EARS-019.example_violation: WHEN the driver presses the lane-keep button, WHERE the Lane Keep Assist feature is installed, THE SYSTEM SHALL activate lane centering.
EARS-019.example_compliant: WHERE the Lane Keep Assist feature is installed, WHEN the driver presses the lane-keep button, THE SYSTEM SHALL activate lane centering within [500 ms].
EARS-019.disambiguation: Applies only once EARS-006 (complex_hybrid_pattern) confirms a hybrid combination is appropriate; this rule checks the ordering of the already-identified clauses.

EARS-020.description: Avoid nesting conditional logic within a trigger or state clause (e.g. an 'IF' inside a 'WHEN' clause); split into separate, linked requirements instead.
trigger_criteria: A trigger or state clause itself contains a further conditional sub-clause (nested IF/WHEN/WHILE) rather than being a single flat condition
EARS-020.example_violation: WHEN the brake pedal is pressed, if the ABS is also active, THE SYSTEM SHALL modulate brake pressure.
EARS-020.example_compliant: WHILE the Anti-lock Braking System is active, WHEN the brake pedal is pressed, THE SYSTEM SHALL modulate brake pressure within [20 ms].
EARS-020.disambiguation: Distinct from EARS-013 (single trigger): EARS-013 flags multiple independent triggers chained together; EARS-020 flags one condition embedded inside another, which should instead be flattened into a hybrid pattern (EARS-006/EARS-019) or split.

EARS-021.description: In unwanted-behavior requirements, state the specific fault/error condition precisely rather than a vague catch-all trigger.
trigger_criteria: The 'IF' clause uses a vague catch-all phrase for the fault condition (e.g. 'if something goes wrong', 'if an error occurs') without naming the specific fault
EARS-021.example_violation: IF something goes wrong with the speed sensor, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL set a fault code.
EARS-021.example_compliant: IF the vehicle speed sensor signal is absent for more than [100 ms], THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL set DTC_101_VSPD_LOSS within [50 ms].
EARS-021.disambiguation: This is the EARS-specific analogue of INCOSE-007 (vague_terms) applied specifically to the IF-trigger clause of the unwanted-behavior pattern.

EARS-022.description: Render EARS keywords (WHEN, IF, THEN, WHILE, WHERE, SHALL) in a consistent case/format (typically upper-case) throughout the requirement set to make patterns machine-parseable.
trigger_criteria: EARS keywords appear in inconsistent case across the requirement set (e.g. 'when' in one requirement, 'WHEN' in another) for the same pattern usage
EARS-022.example_violation: when the ignition is on, the system shall enable the instrument cluster.
EARS-022.example_compliant: WHEN the ignition is ON, THE SYSTEM SHALL enable the instrument cluster within [1 s].
EARS-022.disambiguation: Cross-requirement/style consistency check, evaluated across the set rather than a single statement in isolation; analogous in spirit to INCOSE-036/037 but scoped to EARS keyword formatting specifically.

EARS-023.description: Reserve EARS pattern keywords (WHEN, IF, WHILE, WHERE) for their pattern-defining role; avoid using them elsewhere in a requirement in a way that could be mistaken for a pattern clause.
trigger_criteria: 'when', 'if', 'while', or 'where' appears in the requirement body in an ordinary grammatical sense (e.g. relative/temporal usage) in addition to, or instead of, its EARS pattern role, creating ambiguity about which clause is the actual trigger/state
EARS-023.example_violation: WHEN the fault occurs, THE SYSTEM SHALL log the time when the fault was first detected.
EARS-023.example_compliant: WHEN the fault occurs, THE SYSTEM SHALL log the fault detection timestamp.
EARS-023.disambiguation: Focuses on avoiding a second, informal use of a reserved keyword within the same statement that could be confused with the formal EARS clause it introduces.

EARS-024.description: A state named in a WHILE clause (or feature named in a WHERE clause) must be detectable by the system itself, or by an explicitly identified external means, for the requirement to be verifiable.
trigger_criteria: The WHILE/WHERE condition refers to a state or feature with no stated mechanism (signal, sensor, configuration flag) by which the system or verification process can determine it is true
EARS-024.example_violation: WHILE the road is wet, THE SYSTEM SHALL reduce the traction control threshold.
EARS-024.example_compliant: WHILE the rain sensor indicates precipitation above [threshold], THE SYSTEM SHALL reduce the traction control threshold to [80%] of nominal.
EARS-024.disambiguation: Complements EARS-014; EARS-014 checks that the state concept itself is boundable, while EARS-024 checks that a concrete detection mechanism exists to verify it in practice.

EARS-025.description: Ensure each EARS pattern instance (Ubiquitous, Event-Driven, Unwanted Behavior, State-Driven, Optional Feature, or Complex) maps to exactly one requirement record with exactly one SHALL statement, consistently structured the same way across the requirement set.
trigger_criteria: Multiple SHALL statements are recorded under a single requirement ID
Equivalent pattern types are structured differently across the requirement set (e.g. some event-driven requirements place WHEN first, others bury it mid-sentence)
EARS-025.example_violation: SYS_REQ_0021 contains: 'WHEN X, THE SYSTEM SHALL A. ALSO, THE SYSTEM SHALL B when Y.'
EARS-025.example_compliant: SYS_REQ_0021: 'WHEN X, THE SYSTEM SHALL A within [50 ms].' SYS_REQ_0022: 'WHEN Y, THE SYSTEM SHALL B within [50 ms].'
EARS-025.disambiguation: Cross-requirement structural consistency check, similar in spirit to INCOSE-042 (structured_sets) but focused specifically on one-SHALL-per-record discipline within the EARS pattern framework.
